title: Ezekiel 13
subtitle: Against False Prophets

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Ezekiel

sections.header: Verses 1-16 — Prophets of Peace When There Is No Peace

heb.paragraph: The false prophets 'plaster over' (taphel) a flimsy wall built by the people. The wall represents Israel's false security; the whitewash represents the prophets who say 'peace' when there is no peace. When God sends the storm of judgment, both wall and whitewash will collapse. The metaphor exposes the collaboration between popular delusion and prophetic complicity.

They cry 'shalom, shalom' — but there is no shalom. This echoes Jeremiah 6:14 and 8:11 word for word. Both contemporary prophets identify the same disease: false prophets who anesthetize the nation against God's warnings. Peace-preaching in the face of judgment is not kindness but cruelty.

refs.note: They dress the wound of my people as though it were not serious. 'Peace, peace,' they say, when there is no peace — identical diagnosis from Jeremiah.
Jesus warns of false prophets who come in sheep's clothing — the tradition of prophetic discernment continues.
False prophets will introduce destructive heresies — the NT extends Ezekiel's warning to the church.

notes.note: These prophets follow their own spirit and have seen nothing. The fundamental sin is fabricating messages and attributing them to God.
They have not stood in the gaps or built up the wall — the metaphor of intercessory failure. True prophets both warn and intercede; false prophets do neither.
Leading people astray by saying 'Peace!' when there is no peace. False comfort is more destructive than honest warning.
The hailstones, flooding rain, and violent wind will demolish the whitewashed wall. God himself will tear down the false structure of security.
Calvin warns that the most dangerous false prophets are those within the covenant community who use God's name. External enemies are easily identified; internal deceivers are far more lethal.
Calvin reads the whitewash as flattery: false teachers tell people what they want to hear, coating a rotten structure with attractive words. The storm reveals the truth.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Foxes among ruins — jackals or foxes that scavenge in abandoned buildings. False prophets are parasites on Israel's collapse, not healers of it.
Standing in the gap (perets) is a military metaphor: standing in the breach of a wall to defend the city. The false prophets failed in their defensive duty.
Excluded from the 'council' (sod) of God's people, removed from the register (ketav), and barred from the land — triple excommunication.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block connects false prophecy to the failure of the watchman role (ch. 3): the false prophets are anti-watchmen who see danger coming and declare peace. They represent the institutional corruption of the prophetic office — when the watchman lies, the entire warning system fails.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli identifies the 'wall and whitewash' oracle as belonging to the tradition of prophetic conflict (cf. 1 Kings 22, Micaiah vs. Ahab's prophets). The problem is not that false prophets lack sincerity but that they lack authorization — they speak from their own spirit, not from the divine council.

hist.context: Ezekiel, like Jeremiah, faces opposition from prophets who proclaim peace and prosperity. These are not foreign prophets but Israelite prophets who claim to speak for YHWH. Their oracles come from their own imagination (libbam), not from divine revelation. They are foxes among ruins — scavengers who exploit the disaster rather than repairing the breach. Their failure is specifically described as not 'standing in the gap' (v.5) — they did not intercede for the people.

heb.paragraph: The women sew 'magic bands' (kesatot) on wrists and make veils for heads to 'hunt souls.' The exact nature of these objects is debated — they may be amulets, binding cloths, or divination tools. Whatever the precise practice, it represents the use of magical manipulation to control people, preying on the vulnerable for personal gain.

refs.note: Huldah the prophetess spoke truth from God — the contrast with these false prophetesses is stark.
Paul encounters a slave girl with a divination spirit — the exploitation of the vulnerable through spiritual manipulation continues into the NT.

notes.note: They 'hunt souls' for handfuls of barley and scraps of bread — they sell their spiritual services cheaply, cheapening both the prophetic office and the lives they destroy.
They discouraged the righteous and encouraged the wicked — the exact inversion of the prophetic vocation, which should comfort the afflicted and afflict the comfortable.

Calvin sees the magic practices as evidence that when true prophecy is rejected, superstition fills the vacuum. A society that dismisses God's word inevitably turns to counterfeit spiritual practices. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The kesatot ('magic bands') are hapax legomena — they occur only here in the OT. Proposals include sympathetic magic binding cloths, divinatory wristbands, or snake-charming apparatus. The exact practice is lost.
Profaning God 'among my people for handfuls of barley' — the payment is pitifully small, suggesting these women exploited the poorest members of society.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block argues that the women represent a separate stream of religious practice — magical rather than oracular. While the male false prophets fabricated visions, these women practiced sympathetic magic. Both corrupt the prophetic institution but in different ways.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli connects the 'hunting of souls' to necromantic practices — the women may have claimed to capture and release spirits of the dead. The language of 'keeping alive' and 'putting to death' suggests power over life and death that belongs to God alone.

hist.context: This is one of the rare biblical passages addressing female prophetic practitioners specifically. These women are not condemned for being female prophets (Huldah was a legitimate prophetess, 2 Kgs 22:14) but for practicing divination and magical manipulation. They 'hunt souls' — trapping the living and freeing the guilty. Their magic inverts justice: the righteous suffer and the wicked prosper through their interventions.

rows.label: vv.1-7
rows.text: Indictment: Prophets Who Follow Their Own Spirit

rows.label: vv.8-16
rows.text: Whitewash and the Storm of Judgment

lit.note: Two complementary oracles against false spiritual leadership: male prophets who fabricate visions (vv.1-16) and female practitioners who practice divination (vv.17-23).

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: the chapter is entirely about true vs. false prophecy. Justice at 8: the false prophets invert justice by encouraging the wicked and discouraging the righteous.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: Ezekiel, like Jeremiah, faces opposition from prophets who proclaim peace and prosperity…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Against False Prophets

translations.text: Have you not seen false visions and uttered lying divinations when you say, “The Lord declares,” though I have not spoken?
Have you not seen a false vision and uttered a lying divination, whenever you have said, ‘Declares the LORD,’ although I have not spoken?”
You have not gone up to the breaches in the wall to repair it for the people of Israel so that it will stand firm in the battle on the day of the Lord.
You have not gone up into the breaches, or built up a wall for the house of Israel, that it might stand in battle in the day of the LORD.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "These prophets follow their own spirit and have seen nothing. The fundamental sin is fabricating messages and attributing them to God."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin warns that the most dangerous false prophets are those within the covenant community who use God's name. External enemies are easily identified; internal deceivers are far more lethal."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "Foxes among ruins — jackals or foxes that scavenge in abandoned buildings. False prophets are parasites on Israel's collapse, not healers of it."

thread.text: They dress the wound of my people as though it were not serious. 'Peace, peace,' they say, when there is no peace — identical diagnosis from Jeremiah.
Jesus warns of false prophets who come in sheep's clothing — the tradition of prophetic discernment continues.
False prophets will introduce destructive heresies — the NT extends Ezekiel's warning to the church.
Huldah the prophetess spoke truth from God — the contrast with these false prophetesses is stark.
Paul encounters a slave girl with a divination spirit — the exploitation of the vulnerable through spiritual manipulation continues into the NT.

coaching.tip: False prophets build a flimsy wall and whitewash it (v. 10) — the wall looks solid but can't withstand storm. Jesus uses identical imagery: 'Everyone who hears these words of mine and does not put them into practice is like a foolish man who built his house on sand' (Matthew 7:26). The metaphor of structural deception — appearing sound while being hollow — is Ezekiel's contribution to the prophetic vocabulary of falsehood.

The women who sew 'magic bands' and 'veils' (v. 18) practice divination for hire — 'killing people who should not die and keeping alive people who should not live.' They manipulate spiritual outcomes for profit. Ezekiel condemns the commodification of the sacred — a theme that surfaces in Simon Magus (Acts 8:18–20) and Jesus overturning the money changers. When the holy becomes a product, the prophet intervenes.

prayer_prompt: As God condemns prophets who whitewash flimsy walls and say 'Peace' when there is no peace, consider asking God whether you have been accepting reassurance that feels good but is not true.
